dialogue.utterance: Dispatcher to Unit 3 responding for a 21-year-old male with breathing problems at [CITY], Apt F.
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having progressively worse shortness of breath since yesterday.
Can you tell me your name, today's date, and where we are right now? It sounds like you're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation-AAOx4.
I understand this is an uncomfortable situation-would you mind telling me a bit more?
Your airway appears patent and self-maintained. You're speaking in full sentences without difficulty.
Respirations are 17, non-labored, and with audible expiratory wheezing noted.
Pulse is strong and regular-93 per minute.
Radial pulse is easily palpable bilaterally.
Can you tell me when the shortness of breath first started?
Have you been diagnosed with any medical conditions before?
You mentioned shortness of breath. On a scale of 1 to 10, how bad is it for you today?
How would you describe the feeling when you breathe-like tightness, wheezing, or something else?
Pulse is 93, regular.
Respirations at 17 per minute, non-labored.
Blood pressure is 171 over 87.
SpO₂ is currently 98% on room air.
We won't be measuring glucose in the field based on your stability and protocol.
4-lead ECG is running now to monitor your heart rhythm.
We're going to give you a nebulizer to help with your breathing. Is that okay with you?
Administering Albuterol 5 mg and Ipratropium 0.5 mg via nebulizer, with 8 liters per minute of oxygen.
Given your symptoms and medical history, we are following protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress and asthma exacerbation.
Your pulse has decreased to 83 and is still regular.
SpO₂ improved to 99%.
Your 4-lead EKG is stable and shows a normal sinus rhythm.
I'm going to give you Dexamethasone 10 mg IV to help with your wheezing.
Your skin is normal in color, warm, and dry.
No drainage is present, and your mouth inspection is unremarkable.
Your airway remains stable. No swelling or rigidity in your neck.
Wheezing is noted in all lung fields-consistent with an asthma flare-up.
You have full range of motion in all limbs and your gait is stable.
12-lead EKG: normal findings-no signs of STEMI.
Blood pressure is 143 over 97 now.
Respirations increased to 21-still non-labored, with continuing wheezing.
We are establishing a vein in your left arm for potential medication access if needed.
First IV attempt was unsuccessful, but the IV is now secured in your left forearm.
Repeating 12-lead EKG-no new changes, just normal sinus rhythm.
Your BP has increased slightly to 152 over 89.
We will now place you on the stretcher in a position that is most comfortable.
You are now positioned and covered with a sheet, and secured via five-point restraints.
Could you please share your name, address, and date of birth to assist the hospital?
Have you had any coughing with phlegm or changes in sputum?
Do you have any known medication allergies?
Are you currently taking any medications for chronic conditions?
When was your last time eating or drinking?
Your pulse is back up to 93-very consistent over the past hour.
There hasn't been a visible change in wheezing after Dexamethasone, but your condition isn't worsening.
You'll continue to be monitored the entire way to the hospital.
Your restraints are already in place and adjusted for safety and comfort.
En route, you will remain on the cardiac monitor in case of any arrhythmias.
Your heart rhythm remains stable-nothing to concern on the monitor.
You've chosen [COMPANY_NAME]-next, we will notify the hospital you are en route.
You are now ready for transport. We'll be following protocol for asthma management.
Blood pressure remains stable at 152 over 89 during transport.
Restraints are in place and you are comfortable for safe transport.
You're now ready for transport. We'll be heading to the hospital safely.
SpO₂ remains steady at 99%.
Pulse remains steady at 93 during transport.
All steps of protocol 3-11 have been completed. We're now preparing for handoff.
Patient information has been collected for hospital handoff.
You've arrived and are able to scoot onto the receiving stretcher independently.
You are now in the emergency department. Bed rails are in place for your safety.
Protocol is complete, and care has been passed to the ER team.
You mentioned your breathing has improved. How would you rate your shortness of breath now on a scale of 0 to 10?
